data.title: VFLO: Quality, Value, And Growth In One Basket
data.description:
Introduction To The VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF

The VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF (NASDAQ:VFLO), which has been in existence since June 21, 2023, is backed by Victory Capital- the Texas based global asset management firm. VFLO, which has accumulated AUM of close to $5B in a little over two years, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.39%, and offers an annual yield of 1.45% (VFLO’s distributions are paid out every month) at current prices.

What Does the VFLO ETF Do?

Put simply, VFLO focuses on a basket of 50 US stocks which are known for their relatively high FCF [free cash flow] yields, relatively cheap valuations, and relatively high expected growth rates (on the topline, EBITDA and EPS).

Now, some of you may be wondering what an FCF yield is? Well let’s break that up into two parts- the numerator and the denominator. First you have the FCF or free cash flow (the numerator) which measures the excess cash that a company has left, after accounting for its operating expenses and capital expenses.

In many quarters, this metric is construed to be the epitome of a quality business, as it shows that the company is adroit enough to not only generate ample revenue, but also keep a lid on its costs, and then have sufficient ammunition to either return that excess cash to shareholders (via dividends or share buybacks), grow the business by engaging in mergers & acquisitions, or perhaps reinvest it back into the business through higher research & development. Companies can always make mistakes in how they deploy that free cash (which then becomes a capital allocation problem), but the ability to consistently generate ample free cash year in year out, is a difficult skill that not all of them can manage.

The second part of the FCF yield equation, or the denominator, typically focuses on the market-cap of a stock, but in VFLO’s case they also take into consideration the net debt (long-term debt, and short-term debt, minus the idle cash on the balance sheet), which in essence translates to the enterprise value of a stock. Measuring the free cash flow relative to the total enterprise value is seen as a more comprehensive gauge of how much FCF all investors in VFLO can expect (and not just shareholders).

VFLO doesn’t just randomly pickup up high FCF yielders from the US markets, but goes about its business by attempting to replicate the performance of an index called the Victory U.S. Large Cap Free Cash Flow Index [VULFI], which in turn is created by VettaFi LLC (a fintech subsidiary of the TMX Group Limited) using a rules-based methodology.

VULFI is a subset of the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index [VUEL1I] and in the first step of its construction all financial and REIT stocks are eliminated from consideration. This makes sense as the business models of financial businesses and REITs aren’t incentivized to maintain large amounts of free cash. More so, the accounting impact, particularly with regards to depreciation, can end up providing a misleading picture on the FCF of REITs when one should really be focusing on rental income.

Nonetheless, after this, the largest 400 profitable stocks are chosen even as companies with negative expected FCF and earnings are also weeded out.

What is “expected FCF”? Well, this is a combination of not just the trailing FCF (over the past twelve months) that has been generated by a business, but also the expected FCF (over the next twelve months), which is based on consensus estimates.

In the next stage, the top 75 stocks based on expected FCF yield are chosen, after which this pool is brought down to the final 50 stocks based on the highest growth scores (the average of long-term sales, EBITDA and EPS estimates). The goal here is to weed out stocks which may have high FCF generating ability, but low growth prospects. The final 50 stocks are then weighted on the basis of not just the total FCF, but also the FCF yield. Basically, stocks with a higher quantum of FCF and higher FCF yield, receive the largest weights in this portfolio (although individual security exposure is capped at just 4% so one doesn’t have to worry about any major concentration effects).

What Are The Defining Traits Of VFLO’s Portfolio?

As noted earlier, the base universe of VFLO’s portfolio is the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index [VUEL1I], and the name clearly suggests that this is a large and mid-cap-based index. As a result, note that VFLO’s small-cap exposure is very minimal (less than 2%) with a heavy tilt towards mid-caps (59% of the portfolio). Some of you may wonder why there is even a 2% exposure. Well, while VFLO aims to replicate VULFI, typically only 80% of its net assets are based on the latter. Also between rebalancing dates (which takes place only four times per year), there is the prospect of some stocks transitioning from the mid-cap to small-cap bracket.

However, given that VFLO weighs its stocks on the basis of quantum of free cash flow (besides the FCF yield), we are surprised to not see a higher proportion of giant-cap stocks. Basically, almost two-thirds of this portfolio consists of large and mid-caps.

From a stylistic angle, large and mid-cap “value” stocks dominate the holdings accounting for close to two-thirds of the portfolio. These are stocks that are typically characterized by low price-to-earnings ratios, low price-to-book value ratios, high dividend payouts, etc.

From a sector perspective, we already know that VFLO doesn’t dabble with financials or REITs, so there is the chance of some strong sector exposure towards other sectors. Well, we can see that with two sectors (healthcare and energy) which jointly account for over half the portfolio).

What Are The Risks Associated With VFLO?

For a product that focuses on value stocks and free cash (which serves as the foundation on which large dividend payouts are made), and distributes monthly, VFLO’s dividend yield is quite unappealing (a yield of less than 1.5%, which is over 120bps lower than what the average ETF pays) and thus income investors would do well not to expect the moon.

This could well be on account of it shunning the financial and REIT sectors which are noted for being generous with dividend payouts.

As noted earlier, VFLO’s main exposure is towards the healthcare sector (close to 28% of the portfolio) which remains vulnerable to risks of potential drug price control under the Trump administration going forward. Even if the feasibility of a successful outcome is questionable, discussions around this topic tend to crop up every now and then, triggering unsavory gyrations in the prices of healthcare stocks

Even otherwise its’ worth noting that healthcare stocks aren’t necessarily staring at the most resplendent earnings outlook through FY26. Next year healthcare stocks’ earnings will likely grow at a pace (just 10%) that is lower than most other sectors and the S&P 500 as a whole (14%)

Given the elaborate construction and weighting surrounding not just the trailing FCF, but also forward looking FCF (which is subject to constant changes by the sell-side analyst community which provides the consensus estimates), and VFLO’s individual stock capping of 4%, this portfolio will be prone to constant churn and won’t appeal to those who like to see stability in their portfolios. For context, last year, the annual turnover ratio worked out to a whopping 118% (most ETFs see annual churn of less than 30%), which suggests that a lot of these holdings that VFLO holds aren’t even held for close to a year. With such a high level of annual churn, VFLO is also susceptible to higher transaction costs that could weigh on the overall returns of the fund.

VFLO’s portfolio construction (based on expected FCF) also involves some element of placing faith in forward looking estimates by the sell-side analyst community. These estimates are not always reliable and could end up providing a false picture of things.

Who Is VFLO For?

We view VFLO as suitable for investors who assign credence to the quality factor, stocks with relatively cheap valuations, and decent growth potential, while also desiring a recurring monthly distribution (although this isn’t hugely lucrative, as we’ll touch upon later)

FCF is widely perceived to be an apt barometer of high quality, and its fair to say that the market certainly recognizes that. For the sake of further clarity, note that historically (over a span of 33 years), US stocks who belonged to the lowest quintile from the trailing FCF yield grossly underperformed the S&P 500 (on an annualized basis), whereas those in the highest quintile, outperformed the S&P 500 by over 560bps! In addition to that stocks which score highly on the “expected” FCF yield front, which is what VFLO is oriented towards (where even forward FCF prospects are considered) performed even better.

Then we noted earlier how VFLO tilts toward value style stocks. Well, do consider that VFLO’s portfolio is priced at very compelling valuations, not just on a price-to-cash flow basis (a multiple of less than 8x which translates to a 45% discount over the S&P 500 and the Russell 1000) but also on a price-to-earnings basis (a multiple of ~13x, which is also 45% cheaper than the two benchmarks).

Even though at the heart of it, VFLO is more of a value play, its portfolio construction also involves eradicating low growth (in terms of sales, EBITDA, and EPS) FCF yielders, which also makes it suitable for those who want stocks with respectable growth profiles.

What Are Some Of The Alternatives To FLOW?

Two close peers of VFLO that also construct portfolios on the basis of the FCF yield are the Global X U.S. Cash Flow Kings 100 ETF (FLOW), and the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ).

The latter is the most established and popular product in this space with a listing history of close to nine years, AUM of almost $20B, and the tightest bid-ask spreads of just 0.02% (reflecting the liquidity seen in this ticker. It also covers the widest portfolio of stocks (102) and tilts mainly to large-caps unlike the other two. COWZ also tilts towards the enticing technology sector. However, note that COWZ is the only one of the three that pays dividends on a quarterly basis, and it also comes across as the least cost efficient (an expense ratio of 0.49%). The annual churn of over 150% is also quite concerning.

FLOW appears to be a very low-profile product with less than $25M in AUM and wide bid-ask spreads of 0.19% (it is also the youngest of the three products). However, it deserves some credit for being the most cost efficient, the most lucrative from a yield perspective (like VFLO, it too pays monthly) and also witnesses the lowest degree of churn (although still high by general ETF standards). FLOW also stands out for tilting towards consumer cyclical stocks unlike the other two.

Two areas where VFLO stands out from these alternatives is that it is the only one which also incorporates a forward-looking screening of expected FCF (the other two base the portfolio on trailing FCF), and it also doesn’t take into account REIT stocks.

Summary

VFLO, a narrow portfolio (50 stocks) built around the thesis of expected free cash flow, offers a rare blend of quality, value and growth stocks while also incorporating a useful forward-looking viewpoint and a monthly distribution schedule. While its elaborate construction and sizeable churn may put off investors who appreciate stability, studies have shown that stocks that score highly on the basis of expected FCF, tend to outperform the rest of the pack.

This article answers these three main questions about VFLO:

  • How does VFLO build its portfolio of stocks?
  • Who is VFLO meant for and what are the risks they could face?
  • How does VFLO compare to other funds that use FCF in the selection process?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: The Only Quantum You Can Use Today, But I Would Still Sell
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous breakdown of the quantum computing industry, I argued that the “Holy Grail” general-purpose quantum computer (the one that could crack every encryption and simulate molecules at a scale unimaginable for today’s machines) is still years, maybe decades away. I recommend you read that piece first if you are interested in the industry, because I tried to go deep into how it all works and why we may never see this stronger-than-all computer. In this article, I will talk about annealing and the gate model. The previous piece described the gate model in detail, while this one will focus on annealing.

D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) is the only public pure-play quantum company that is actually generating revenue today from its customers. But it’s not building the type of quantum computers most people imagine when they hear the word “quantum.” Instead, it focuses on a very different approach (quantum annealing), which is something entirely different from what I explained yesterday. It has different technology, commercial potential, and as a result, the stock will behave slightly differently.

Annealing vs. Gate Model

Most of the names I discussed in my previous piece, like IBM (IBM), Google (GOOGL), IonQ (IONQ), and Quantinuum (HON), are focused on building gate-model quantum computers. These are the machines that, once error-corrected (something I called logical qubits in the last article) and scaled to hundreds of thousands of qubits, could revolutionize cryptography, chemistry, and countless other fields. They are basically building that imaginary quantum computer that will be stronger than all of the computers in the world combined and will be hacking into every encryption. But there is a tiny problem. Nobody is even remotely close to that, and there are no guarantees someone ever will be. It’s not that we are years away. If some major technological or scientific breakthrough doesn’t happen soon, we could be decades away.

D-Wave, however, uses quantum annealing, which is entirely different, and this is the reason it can start commercial operations already today. It is quantum-powered, but it’s not what you initially imagine when you hear quantum computer, and it never will be. The whole concept is much simpler: you start with qubits in a kind of “fluid” state, and instead of manipulating them step by step like in the gate model (manipulating them is the biggest challenge), you let the system naturally settle into the lowest-energy configuration.

That lowest-energy state often represents the best solution to an optimization problem. It could solve a problem of routing trucks across a city or scheduling thousands of workers across shifts. In general, it’s problems where there are millions of possible combinations, but one or two that are more efficient.

So D-Wave isn’t trying to solve all of quantum’s promises. Its focus is on optimization, logistics, some machine learning applications, and simulations. Management stopped at the beginning of the latest call to insist that annealing isn’t a niche approach, and on this point, they’re right. But there were a couple of points where their explanation is stretched. In general, I wouldn’t take a word from an interested party for the whole truth, but it relates in general to all public companies and their managers. Let’s see the quote first, and then discuss it:

Before turning to commercial updates, I wanted to take a moment to remind everyone of the differences between annealing and gate model, as there still appears to be continued misinformation that we believe is confusing the market.

Annealing and gate model are different types of quantum computing approaches that solve different types of problems. According to peer-reviewed research, gate model quantum computers will not offer advantages for all problem types. Multiple research results have shown that annealing quantum computers outperform and are expected to continue to outperform gate model on optimization problems. So gate model cannot universally solve all problems better than classical.

It is also important to understand that annealing is not a niche solution. We believe that annealing quantum computing is well suited to a broad set of problems, including AI machine learning, quantum simulation and business optimization, which is ubiquitous in today's modern enterprise. Business optimization problems encompass things like workforce scheduling, production scheduling, resource allocation, vehicle routing and so on.

Together, these represent extensive use cases with far-reaching potential. To characterize annealing quantum computing as niche is misleading and ill-informed. Both annealing and gate model quantum computers can solve a broad range of problems and each has its limitations with problems it cannot efficiently solve.

Annealing quantum computing is very good at solving optimization problems, which cannot be efficiently solved by gate model. Gate model quantum computers, once commercialized, are expected to be very good at quantum chemistry and 3D fluid dynamics, which cannot be efficiently solved by annealing. And both systems, both systems can tackle linear algebra and factorization, meaning problems related to AI, machine learning and cryptography. We believe that organizations will need both annealing and gate model systems in order to address their full problem sets. This is why D-Wave is building both types of quantum computers for our customers.

What the management is right about is that it’s not really “niche.” The optimization is everywhere, and it’s crucial for many huge, global industries. The idea that annealing could become the specialized tool that businesses use makes sense. If gate-model quantum computers eventually arrive at scale, they could replace the annealing, but it’s very early to talk about it. There will be many important details, like scaling, cost-effectiveness, use cases, etc.

Where I think management stretches the story is in how fast this coexistence becomes commercially meaningful. They’re right that optimization problems are everywhere, but the difference between quantum computers being theoretically better at this than regular computers, and someone actually ready to pay hundreds of millions for it, is huge. One of the major concerns in the quantum industry is whether it will be cost-effective.

What is interesting is that management, while protecting annealing, said they are working on both. So it means they recognize the need to work on the gate model. What management did not say is that their efforts on this are far behind competitors like IBM, Google, and Quantinuum. It’s a big and very expensive problem to solve. I described it in the previous piece.

A Working Product in the Market

But short term, there are many advantages to what D-Wave is doing. Unlike IonQ or Rigetti (RGTI), which are still mostly research-driven with cloud demos, D-Wave’s Advantage2 system is already commercially available. Customers can buy access through the cloud or even install on-premise systems, as the Julich Supercomputing Center in Germany has done.

Advantage2 has shown meaningful technical progress over the previous generation, with double coherence times, 40% more energy scale, and higher connectivity between qubits. D-Wave claims to have achieved a kind of “quantum supremacy” over regular computers. Whether D-Wave’s claims of supremacy will stand the test of time is still up for debate, but the direction looks good. They have customers like E.ON, GE Vernova, Nikon, and NTT Data, and even academic and government partners like the U.K.’s National Quantum Computing Center and the University of Oxford. It has also built proofs of concept in aerospace, where a Fortune 500 client is testing a dozen optimization cases, and even in law enforcement, where North Wales Police used D-Wave’s system to optimize patrol car deployment.

Financial Results

In the latest quarter, D-Wave reported $3.1 million in revenue, up 42% year over year. Bookings reached $1.3 million, almost double the prior year’s quarter, and gross margins is pretty high at over 60%.

GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 63.8%, representing a slight improvement from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 GAAP gross margin of 63.6%. Non-GAAP gross margin for the second quarter was 71.8%, a slight decrease of 1.3% from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 non-GAAP gross margin of 73.1%.

But the losses are much bigger. On a GAAP basis, the company has a net loss of $167 million, though most of that came from warrant revaluation. On an adjusted basis, the loss was closer to $25 million, which still reflects the heavy cost of R&D and scaling. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $20 million for the quarter.

Net loss for the second quarter was $167.3 million or $0.55 per share, an increase of $149.5 million or $0.45 per share from the second quarter of fiscal 2024 net loss of $17.8 million or $0.10 a share. The increase in the net loss was primarily due to $142 million in noncash nonoperating charges related to the remeasurement of the company's warrant liability as well as realized losses stemming from actual warrant exercises.

The one bright spot is the balance sheet, but investors won’t really like that. D-Wave now holds over $800 million in cash, its strongest liquidity position ever. That came at the cost of heavy dilution. Shares outstanding have increased significantly from around 127 million at the end of 2022 to over 340 million today. Investors who bought earlier have already paid for this liquidity.

Valuation is extraordinary to put lightly. With a market cap of nearly $9.5 billion and revenues barely over $15 million for the first half of 2025, D-Wave trades at over 350x forward sales. For context, that’s more expensive than all of the Nvidia’s, Palantirs, and whatever expensive stock you can think of during the AI hype. This stock is not valued like a confident player it could become in optimization, but like a company that created the first quantum computer and took over the world with it. In the past. And now it’s just owns everything.

Jokes aside, I don’t think investors understand what they are actually buying if they drive this stock to a high valuation like this. And I don’t blame them. Quantum computing is this magical unicorn no one understands, but also almost no one is trying to. I have read so many articles and valuations of different quantum stocks and the sector as a whole, but I realized people are not talking about what we really invest in. There is no discussion of whether what they are waiting for is really possible to achieve. It doesn’t, by the way, mean I did not like D-Wave. It’s an interesting company. But with their roadmap as of today, I don’t think they would be fairly valued at this level if they have already achieved everything planned for the next few years.

Future Possibilities

The bullish story around D-Wave really comes down to a few things. First, they have a commercial head start. No one has that really yet. D-Wave is already selling systems and offering cloud access to customers today.

Second, the management has said that they are also working on a gate model. I am not sure if it's that great of idea given how far they are behind, but if they pull that off together with the annealing, they could create a very unusual position in the sector. And third, the U.S. government, Japan, South Korea, and parts of Europe have already opened the door to partnerships. Defense, logistics, and energy optimization are exactly the kind of problems annealing is well-suited to, and if those institutions decide this technology is worth backing, D-Wave could benefit disproportionately.

But there’s a bearish side as well. Annealing may turn out to be too narrow in scope. Or not cost-effective. If gate-model systems begin scaling, they could replace annealing altogether. And even if both approaches do coexist, D-Wave is years behind in gate-model, competing against giants like IBM, Google, and Microsoft with their huge R&D budgets.

The company survives by selling more stock, and that comes at the expense of existing shareholders. Finally, the valuation is already so high as if D-Wave will dominate optimization and stay relevant in gate-model at the same time.

Conclusion

D-Wave is not “that” quantum computer. Not the one that will crack RSA encryption or model new drugs. But it is a quantum company (not a computer) with a product on the market today.

For investors, the trade-off is clear. On the one hand, there is real technology, real customers, and a lot of cash. On the other hand, there is a $9.5 billion market cap against tiny revenues, intense dilution, and the huge risk that annealing turns out to be a technological dead-end.

If you’re bullish, well, I think you are very brave to buy it now. If you’re bearish, you see this speculative bubble priced decades ahead of its time. For me, it’s a fascinating company to follow, a pioneer in proving that quantum can deliver value today, but at these valuations, it’s a lottery ticket rather than an investment.

Note: I never short stocks, and I wouldn’t start with something that’s already up more than 2,000% in a year. My Sell rating simply means that if I had been holding it from earlier, I would be taking profits now.

data.title: India's Hidden Reform: Why U.S. Investors May Be Missing The Real Story
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The Headline Problem

If you're a U.S. investor paying attention to India in September 2025, chances are you've seen only one storyline: geopolitics. India buys discounted oil from Russia. Prime Minister Modi is seen shaking hands with Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping. There's discomfort in Washington about whether India is truly a partner or just playing all sides.1

This is the dominant narrative, reinforced by soundbites, often with a skeptical undertone. For many investors, it breeds hesitation. If the world is fracturing into blocs, why take exposure in a country that appears "non-aligned"?

But focusing on this alone risks missing the far bigger story. Because while U.S. audiences hear about geopolitics, what they're not hearing about is something far more consequential for investors: India's Next-Gen GST Reform.2

The Missed Reform Story

Ahead of the Diwali festival in late October 2025, the Indian government will roll out sweeping reforms to its Goods and Services Tax (GST), the country's unified consumption tax, akin in scope to a national value-added tax (VAT).

The changes weren't subtle. Figure 1 is a summary document prepared by India's government to detail the high-level changes.

Figure 1: India's Next-Gen GST Reform

Source: Government of India, Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, "Next-Gen GST reform: Historic Diwali gift for the nation" [infographic], CBIC, 2025. Ghee is a type of clarified butter. Namkeens, Bhujia & Mixtures is a broad category of traditional Indian salty snacks. LPG is liquified petroleum gas. CNG is compressed natural gas. cc stands for cubic centimeter. mm stands for millimeters. LED stands for light-emitting diode. LCD stands for liquid crystal display. 2.5 Lakh rupees per month is 250,000 rupees, and is roughly similar in value to $3,000, as of September 5, 2025. MSMEs stands for micro, small and medium enterprises.

Looking just at the six headers in figure 1, the government's intent comes into sharper focus. Three of the categories—Daily Essentials, Farmers & Agriculture and Healthcare—are all being pushed toward a 5% GST rate. That signals a deliberate policy choice to lower the cost of necessities for households, farmers and patients. These are the foundations of day-to-day life: food, hygiene, farming inputs and medical care. By making them cheaper, the government is clearly prioritizing broad affordability, household consumption and support for small producers and rural communities.

At the same time, two other categories, Automobiles and Electronic Appliances, are being normalized to an 18% rate, down from the punitive 28% that had long discouraged demand. This creates breathing room for middle-class consumers and could accelerate spending on durable goods. The sixth category, Affordable Education, goes even further, dropping rates to nil. That underscores education as a public good rather than a taxable luxury, placing it on par with zero-rated essentials. Taken together, the six headers show a government that is easing pressure on both the household wallet and the engines of small enterprise, while also giving a symbolic and material boost to human capital investment through free-of-tax education.

India's Real Story Isn't Geopolitics; It's the Consumption Revival

The GST reform immediately expands household spending power. Roughly 99% of goods that are currently taxed at 12% are moving to 5%, and about 90% of goods currently taxed at 28% are moving to 18%.3 That means essentials, health care, educational items and even aspirational durables become significantly cheaper for 1.4 billion people. In a country where ~60% of GDP is consumption-led, this is not a marginal adjustment; it is a direct injection of demand into the system.4 First-order effects show up in household wallets, rural affordability for tractors and farm inputs and urban budgets freed up for consumer goods and services.

The second-order effects are already visible in earnings momentum and market positioning. Consumer staples, which had suffered two years of downgrades, just posted their strongest revenue surprises in five quarters. Autos rallied 4% in a single day after the GST announcement, with real estate, metals and staples following closely behind. Domestic mutual funds, still structurally under-weight in consumer staples by around -130 basis points relative to their benchmarks, are beginning to raise allocations, while foreign selling pressure in staples has finally stabilized. That creates both a fundamental and flow-driven opportunity: earnings revisions in consumer sectors are turning upward at the same time positioning is light, a combination that often leads to sustained outperformance.5

The third-order implications are macroeconomic and structural. Inflation is expected to moderate to around 3% in FY2026,6 and the current account deficit is contained at ~1% of gross domestic product (GDP), conditions that allow India to sustain this consumption push without destabilizing its balance sheet. Crucially, rural demand is strengthening on the back of a favorable monsoon season, which delivers the bulk of India's annual rainfall between June and September. That rainfall replenishes water supplies and is vital for farming, since nearly half of India's agricultural land is still rain-fed. Early data shows monsoon precipitation tracking 7% above normal this year. That has translated into stronger kharif sowing, the summer planting season that covers staple crops like rice, pulses and coarse cereals, up 3.4% year-on-year. More rain and more planting mean better harvests, rising farm incomes and healthier demand from rural India, which represents more than a third of consumption. As cheaper goods lift volumes and rural incomes improve, private-sector capex begins to follow, already visible in housing sales and new manufacturing projects. The result is a reinforcing cycle: policy lowers costs, demand expands, earnings and employment strengthen, and investment accelerates. For equity markets, this is the story U.S. investors are missing: India's domestic engine is firing in sync across households, corporates and the policy backdrop.7

Conclusion: India's Place in a Diverging Emerging Market Landscape8

Looking across emerging markets year-to-date, leadership has been dominated by smaller, idiosyncratic stories: Colombia up roughly 77%, Greece not far behind, and the Czech Republic up roughly 57%. Much of this strength reflects local rebounds or commodity-driven surges that may not be durable. By contrast, larger emerging markets show a more mixed picture: China is up nearly 39% year-to-date, but its longer-term record is uneven, with a five-year annualized return in negative territory, actually the worst over that period of all 24 indexes shown in figure 2. Brazil is approaching 40% this year, but its 10-year track record is a more modest 8.8%. The key point: many of the best short-term performers as of early September 2025 currently lack the long-term consistency that institutional allocators may find more comforting.

India, in contrast, looks disappointing if judged purely on 2025 year-to-date performance, sitting near the bottom of the list at –1.95%, the worst of the still-positive performers over the period. But zoom out and the picture changes dramatically: over three years, India is up 9.96% annualized, over five years, 13.4%, and over ten years, 9.6%. These figures put India firmly in the upper tier of sustained performance among large emerging markets. Unlike commodity-dependent peers or reform-fragile economies, India's equity returns reflect a durable base of domestic consumption, steady policy reform and a corporate sector that has consistently delivered through cycles. What looks like near-term underperformance is more accurately a pause within a long-term compounding story.

The strategic implication is clear. U.S. investors focusing only on headlines, India's geopolitical positioning or a single year of lagging equity returns risk missing the structural opportunity. GST reform, fiscal discipline, favorable demographics and rising rural demand all point to a consumption engine that is strengthening just as other emerging markets may face greater fragility. In a world where many EM allocations are forced into volatile "boom-bust" cycles, India offers a rare combination: short-term noise, long-term consistency. That consistency is precisely what sets it up to remain a core emerging market allocation and potentially the anchor for investors seeking exposure to the world's fastest-growing large economy.

Figure 2: Why India's Equity Story Can't Be Judged by 2025

Source: MSCI, with performance data measured as of 9/19/25. Past performance is not indicative of future returns. You cannot invest directly in an index.

1 Source: "Xi Jinping's anti-American party," The Economist, 9/2/25.

2 Source: Unless otherwise noted, the source for different GST information is Government of India, Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, "Next-Gen GST reform: Historic Diwali gift for the nation" [infographic], CBIC, 2025.

3 Source: "GST reforms: A potential boost to domestic consumer-sensitives" (India Strategy report), Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research, 8/21/25.

4 Source: "India economics – Catalysts in place for stronger growth" (investor presentation), Morgan Stanley Research, 9/4/25.

5 Source: Goldman Sachs, 2025.

6 In India, the fiscal year (FY) runs from April 1 to March 31 of the following year. FY2026 = April 1, 2025, → March 31, 2026.

7 Source for economic data in this paragraph is Morgan Stanley, 2025.

8 Source for this section is MSCI, with performance data measured as of 9/4/25.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: Trikafta And Alyftrek Cement Market Leadership
data.description:
After Vertex Pharmaceuticals' stock price (NASDAQ:VRTX) reached its all-time high on March 14, 2025, the bulls' influence began to weaken, and the "bears", in turn, began to take control.

In my opinion, the 25.8% decline in its share price in just six months is only partially justified for reasons I'll discuss later.

However, overall, Wall Street's attitude toward Vertex is diverging from the significant progress I've seen in developing its cystic fibrosis portfolio since 2022.

While I'll discuss this franchise in detail later, its 'crown jewel' is, of course, Alyftrek, and it's thanks to it that I believe Vertex's risk/reward profile is becoming more attractive than ever.

The first milestone I want to highlight is that on July 1, the European Commission approved Alyftrek for the treatment of certain patients over 6 years of age with CF [cystic fibrosis], in part because it was more effective than Kaftrio.

A second, significant achievement for Vertex was reaching an agreement with NHS England on reimbursement for this triple combination medication, which will allow people living in England [approximately 11,100 have been diagnosed with CF] to access it as early as mid-July.

Let's continue.

Vertex's cystic fibrosis franchise again beats Wall Street expectations

Vertex's revenue was $2.96 billion in the second quarter of 2025, beating the consensus forecast by $58 million and my base case scenario by $45 million.

Digging deeper into the numbers, it grew 11.7% year-on-year and 6.9% quarter-on-quarter despite a lackluster performance from Journavx [suzetrigine], which Vertex had high hopes for as a future replacement for opioid medications [morphine, oxycodone, and fentanyl], the "gold standard" in the treatment of acute pain.

So, sales of this NaV1.8 inhibitor reached $12 million, increasing at first glance by a stunning 823.1% quarter-on-quarter, but missing my expectations by $8 million.

But that's not all the disappointment I've seen with Vertex's non-opioid pain franchise over the last 2 months.

First, Reshma Kewalramani, who is the CEO, said on the quarterly earnings call that Vertex is delaying the start of a pivotal clinical trial that would evaluate the efficacy of Journavx in treating lumbosacral radiculopathy [pain in the lower back] after receiving comments from the FDA.

First, in peripheral neuropathic pain or PNP, we had a productive end of Phase II meeting with the FDA on our PNP program. While a broad PNP label remains our goal, at this time, the FDA does not see a path to a broad indication. As such, we will not be initiating an LSR trial at present.

The second setback that I highlight is the press release of August 4.

In it, Vertex reported that VX-993 [also NaV1.8 inhibitor], the successor to Journavx, failed to meet its primary endpoint in treating acute pain after bunion surgery, either at its lowest [10 mg first dose] or highest dose [180 mg first dose].

Let's move on.

Vertex's non-GAAP EPS beat my upside scenario by 12 cents, reaching $4.52.

Thus, it grew by 11.3% quarter-on-quarter despite an increase in Vertex's SG&A expenses from $396.4 million in Q1 2025 to $424.6 million in Q2 2025.

Why did these costs rise?

The key reason I highlight is the launch of Journavx, which I discussed earlier, as well as Casgevy, a one-time gene therapy being developed jointly with CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) to treat two types of blood diseases, namely transfusion-dependent beta thalassemia [approved by the FDA on January 16, 2024] and sickle cell disease [approved on December 8, 2023].

On the other hand, I don't think it makes sense to discuss Vertex's year-over-year earnings growth since it acquired Alpine on April 10, 2024, for $4.9 billion, and thus its net income was negative in Q2 last year and equal to about -$3.59 billion.

Now it's time to answer an equally important question that I always ask myself.

Which of Vertex's medications made key contributions to its EBIT and revenue growth?

To answer this question, Vertex's financial position, in my opinion, continues to strengthen, obviously due to the single fact that it is the undisputed leader in the cystic fibrosis therapeutics market, especially after AbbVie's hopeful called ABBV-119 failed.

More precisely, AbbVie (ABBV) announced the failure of ABBV-119 on its Q1 2022 earnings call.

In our cystic fibrosis program, we recently completed an interim analysis of a Phase II proof-of-concept study evaluating our triple combination therapy. The results – the efficacy results from this interim analysis did not meet our prespecified criteria for advancing this triple therapy in development.

This study was designed with a 28-day run-in treatment period, with a dual combination therapy containing our C1 corrector and potentiator, followed by a 28-day treatment period, with a triple combination, which included the addition of our C2 corrector, ABBV-119.

This allowed us to independently assess the therapeutic potential of our C2 corrector. The results showed that the addition of 119 did not provide a meaningful improvement in FEV1 or reduction in sweat chloride concentration over our dual combination therapy.

Since those distant days, I haven't heard any significant progress from AbbVie in developing its cystic fibrosis portfolio, except for its sale of ABBV-2222 [Phase 2 completed, NCT03969888], ABBV-3067, and ABBV-2851 to Sionna Therapeutics (SION) on July 16, 2024.

I'll continue with a discussion of Vertex's portfolio.

Vertex continues to thrive primarily thanks to Alyftrek and Trikafta. Each of these two medications has three active ingredients, namely deutivacaftor/tezacaftor/vanzacaftor and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor, respectively.

However, already at this stage of the analysis, I want to point out that the key difference between them is that, thanks to deutivacaftor, which in turn is a modification of ivacaftor [the hydrogen atoms are replaced by its isotope called deuterium], Alyftrek is taken only once a day and not twice, as is the case with Trikafta.

But that's not all I wanted to say before I move on to discussing their performance in Q2 2025.

On February 8, 2024, Vertex published data from the SKYLINE 102 and SKYLINE 103 clinical trials, in which it did what I believe is absolutely right, nnamely,it directly compared the efficacy of Alyftrek and its "cash cow," Trikafta, in treating people with cystic fibrosis.

Although Alyftrek was no less effective than Trikafta in improving lung function [LS mean difference was 0.2 in both studies], to my surprise, it demonstrated a clinically meaningful improvement in the function of a transmembrane protein called CFTR, which ultimately led to a substantial reduction in sweat chloride levels [+0.9 mmol/L vs. ‑7.5 mmol/L; P<0.0001].

I'm done with the scientific jargon and discussion of clinical data.

But one more question remains. Why does Vertex need Alyftrek?

In my opinion, the first reason is that the US basic patent for Trikafta expires in 2037, and for Alyftrek in 2039.

The second reason is Vertex's attempt to pay Royalty Pharma (RPRX) less [4% or 8% royalty rate for Alyftrek and 9% for Trikafta].

I'd like to remind you that Royalty Pharma acquired the royalty rights from sales of Vertex's cystic fibrosis franchise from CFFT for $3.3 billion on November 19, 2014.

And here I should also clarify, since Royalty disagrees with Vertex's actions.

In my opinion, this is quite logical, since Alyftrek is the successor to Trikafta. Both medications target the same mutation called F508del, and, as I mentioned earlier, ivacaftor and deutivacaftor have almost identical molecular structures.

Overall, during the quarterly earnings call, Terrance Coyne, the CFO of Royalty Pharma, stated the following.

Now before I hand it over to Pablo, I should also note that we did not receive from Vertex the full royalty to which we are entitled on Alyftrek, specifically the royalty related to deuterated ivacaftor. As we have previously stated, we believe we are entitled to a royalty of 8% on sales of Alyftrek. However, Vertex only paid us a royalty of 4%.

As a result, we commenced the dispute resolution process contemplated by the agreements relating to our royalties on Vertex's cystic fibrosis products. That process is subject to confidentiality obligations and so we do not expect to provide any updates until the matter is resolved, which we anticipate by around the end of 2026. We continue to receive our full royalties on Trikafta, Kalydeco, Symdeko and Orkambi.

Let's continue.

Alyftrek sales reached $156.8 million in Q2 2025, up 190.9% quarter-on-quarter, partly due to switching patients from Trikafta and less effective FDA/EMA-approved cystic fibrosis medications, among which I highlight Kalydeco, Orkambi, and Symdeko/Symkevi.

Risks

In the table below, I've listed Vertex-related risks, including the R&D failure to develop VX-993, a dispute with Royalty Pharma over royalty rates on Alyftrek sales, and the lack of strong diversification of its pipeline of product candidates, which, in my opinion, are primarily aimed at treating cystic fibrosis.

Takeaway

Yes, Vertex is a growth stock and therefore reacts sharply to any R&D setbacks.

As I mentioned earlier in the article, the "dark spots" in its portfolio are, of course, the failure of VX-993 in Phase 2, and Journavx's sales growth rate is currently not high enough for Wall Street to call it a "new blockbuster" in the pain management therapeutics market.

However, despite this, Vertex maintains its full-year 2025 revenue guidance of $11.85-$12 billion, which implies 7.7% year-over-year growth.

But more importantly, if I look at Vertex from a slightly different angle, and also given its current share price of $378.5, its non-GAAP P/E ratio will decline from 20.89x in 2025 to 13.4x in 2029, which is below its 5-year average by 18.7% and 39.5%, respectively.

Thanks to this, among other things, I am starting to cover Vertex with a 'Buy' rating.

data.title: Yara International: A Hidden Food Security Powerhouse Trading At Just 5x EV/EBITDA
data.description:
Yara International (OTCPK:YARIY) is one of the world’s largest fertilizer producers, operating plants across 60+ countries and supplying crop nutrition to over 150 markets. The company plays a critical role in global food security and is investing in clean ammonia and low-carbon fertilizer solutions, positioning itself to benefit from the global shift toward sustainable agriculture. However, its energy-intensive operations leave margins sensitive to natural gas prices and cyclical fertilizer demand. In this report, I initiate coverage of Yara and assess how its production footprint, exposure to energy markets, and sustainability strategy shape the investment opportunity for long-term shareholders.

Yara’s Success Is Underpinned By Food Security

Yara is not a company that is completely unfamiliar to me; I just never covered it before. Yara has its biggest plant in Sluiskil (The Netherlands), near Terneuzen, where I was born. For Yara, the simple investment case hinges fully on global food security, and from there we need to expand that with the opportunities, risks, and the financial framework.

The world’s population is growing and is expected to reach 9.6 billion by 2050, according to estimates from the United Nations. Currently, there are about 8.2 billion people, indicating that in the coming 25 years, the world’s population will grow 17%. Together with global economic growth, this will increase demand for food production. At the same time, cropland area growth is expected to taper due to soil degradation, climate change, and urban expansion. Expectations are that arable area will only grow by 0.5% by 2050. That requires the productivity of cropland to increase. So, that requires fertilizers to improve yields, and that is where Yara comes in with their fertilizers. It should be noted, however, that part of the cropland growth stagnation is driven by higher yield per hectare. So, there is a feedback loop driven by fertilizers on cropland use. Nevertheless, it is clear that fertilizers play a key role in improving yields to support food security. Especially in areas of the world where populations are set to expand fastest, fertilizers have been underused, providing a significant growth opportunity for Yara. Stagnation in crop land area against increasing food production demand in support of a growing population and growing middle class is the pillar of the investment case for Yara.

The investment case, of course, also has risks, and that may not be centered so much on slowing demand, but it is more driven by feedstock and energy costs. Yara uses natural gas as an energy source, but it also serves as input in the stream methane reforming process to turn methane into hydrogen to make ammonia, which is a key building block for nitrogen fertilizers.

Natural gas prices are currently high, and when it comes to emissions, we note that Yara has been marked as one of the top emitters of carbon dioxide in the Netherlands, where emission regulations are tightening and squeezing the chemical industry next to the high energy prices. So, the company may face further cost pressures due to that but could offset this by introducing more sustainable hydrogen production facilities. In terms of supply/demand, we note that China is significantly overproducing fertilizers, which is a margin threat to Yara. However, as fertilizers are critical to food production and with high crop prices, it is easier for Yara to pass higher input costs to the customers.

This also puts the company in a rather strong position not only for agricultural demand but also for the energy transition, which includes the use of clean hydrogen.

Yara’s Market And Operational Levers In Play

During the second quarter, Yara grew revenues from $3.53 billion to $3.95 billion, indicating growth of around 12%, while deliveries increased 1%. EBITDA grew from $513 million to $652 million, indicating a 27% increase and margin expansion from 6.1% to 9.9%. Volume and mix changes were neutral to EBITDA, but the company was able to realize $105 million in margin growth, which is strong, as there was a $135 million headwind from higher energy costs. The company is also working on structural fixed-cost savings to reduce annual costs by $150 million.

The second quarter results showed the strength of Yara as a provider of essential products, giving it pricing power and pass-through capabilities while also reducing fixed costs.

Yara International Has Upside But With Challenges

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I have assessed Yara International stock using the figures for the listing in Oslo. That means the price targets in the image above have to be divided by two to get the price target for the YARIY ticker. The investment case for Yara almost fully hinges on the discount it trades at compared to its median EV/EBITDA valuation. When it comes to EBITDA, 2025 is expected to be significantly better than last year, and the same holds for free cash flow. However, in the years after, EBITDA is expected to be modestly lower at around $2.5 billion, while free cash flow is expected to decrease as capital expenditures tick up. So, the upside we currently see is merely to close the gap against the historical valuation multiples, but beyond that, we currently don’t see much upside. The flattish EBITDA performance between 2025 and 2027 may be a reason why the stock will underperform, but I believe that from a valuation perspective, there is substantial upside to justify a buy rating to close the gap between the current EV/EBITDA of 5.1x and the historical levels of 7.6x.

Conclusion: Yara International Has A Nice Business Model, But Earnings Growth Is Challenged

I believe that there definitely is an investment case for Yara that is driven by long-term fundamentals on food security. However, at the same time, we do note that earnings growth is basically nonexistent beyond 2025, and that is likely driven by feedstock prices and competition. So, I mark the stock a "Buy" not for its earnings growth but for its depressed EV/EBITDA multiple.

data.title: How Does The Federal Reserve Set Interest Rates?
data.description:
By Lydia Mashburn Newman, Paul Mueller

Introduction

People in financial markets and in the media regularly track Federal Reserve announcements and forecast what the Fed’s interest rate target will be in the future. But when people speak of the Federal Reserve increasing or decreasing the interest rate, to what are they referring? What interest rates does the Fed target? How does the Fed target them? What are the effects of its interest rate targets? This Explainer addresses those questions.

What the Fed Targets

The Federal Reserve targets an interest rate called the federal funds rate (FFR). The FFR represents the interest rate banks charge one another for overnight borrowing of reserves in the federal funds market. Reserves are money. They include cash that banks keep on hand as well as the money banks hold in accounts at the Fed.

Banks lend and borrow reserves in the federal funds market based on their short-term money needs—these include money to meet depositor withdrawals, to pay out loans made to consumers, and to cover regulatory requirements. How many reserves banks lend or borrow depends on how much money they need to meet these liquidity obligations. Banks with extra reserves lend; those with shortfalls borrow. The amount of lending and borrowing of reserves in the federal funds market affects the FFR.

The Fed can influence this market and the FFR by adding newly created reserves or extracting reserves from the banking system. When the Fed creates more reserves, the FFR falls. This is called expansionary monetary policy because it stimulates economic activity. When the Fed reduces the supply of reserves, the FFR tends to rise. This is called contractionary monetary policy because it reduces available credit and dampens economic activity.

For most of its history, until 2008, the Federal Reserve influenced the FFR market by adding reserves to, or subtracting reserves from, the banking system through open market operations. These operations involved the Fed buying bonds with newly created bank reserves, thereby lowering the FFR, or selling bonds and extinguishing bank reserves, thereby increasing the FFR. Since reserves were relatively scarce in the pre-crisis system, even small interventions by the Fed had predictable effects on the FFR.

In modern times, rather than using open market operations, the Fed relies on three interest rates that it directly sets, or administers, to increase or decrease reserves in the banking system. Through these rates, the Fed influences banks’ borrowing and lending activity in the FFR market. The result is an FFR within the Fed’s interest rate target.

How the Fed Targets the FFR

The three rates the Fed sets to reach its FFR target range are: the discount rate, interest on reserve balances (IORB), and overnight reverse repurchase agreements (ON RRP). The most important are IORB and ON RRP.

IORB is the primary tool the Fed uses to achieve its FFR target. It is the rate the Fed pays banks for reserves kept on deposit at the Fed. For contractionary monetary policy, the Fed sets this rate above what banks can typically earn elsewhere, encouraging them to keep excess reserves at the Fed rather than lending them, thereby exerting upward pressure on the FFR. For expansionary monetary policy, the Fed sets this rate below what banks can earn elsewhere, encouraging them to put more reserves to use in the economy, thereby reducing the FFR.

The ON RRP is the lower bound of the Fed’s FFR target range. It is used to reinforce the Fed’s IORB rate by offering a similar, but lower, interest rate to non-bank financial institutions such as hedge funds, money market mutual funds, insurance companies, and government-sponsored enterprises. These institutions cannot earn IORB because they cannot keep reserves at the Fed. However, they can influence overnight lending markets with their own reserves. Therefore, the Fed offers ON RRP to non-banks to ensure these institutions keep in line with the Fed’s FFR target.

The discount rate is set above IORB and marks the high end of the Fed’s FFR target range. It is the rate that the Fed charges banks for borrowing directly from the Fed at the discount window. Since no bank would pay more than this rate to get reserves, it creates a ceiling on the Fed’s FFR target. Historically, borrowing directly from the Fed carried significant stigma – people in the market interpreted it to mean the bank was high-risk, distressed, or both. Since becoming part of the Fed’s interest rate setting system, however, the discount window stigma has declined considerably.

Through August 2025, the Fed’s target FFR range was 4.25–4.5 percent. To achieve its target, the Fed set IORB at 4.4 percent, ON RRP at 4.25 percent, and the discount rate at 4.5 percent. As a result, the effective FFR has landed roughly in the middle of IORB and the ON RRP at 4.33 percent throughout 2025 (Figure 1).

Figure 1: Fed Interest Rate Targets Since 2023

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED Graph

How Interest Rate Targets Interact with the Fed’s Balance Sheet and Banks

While adjusting IORB and ON RRP allows the Fed to manage the FFR in what appears to be a precise fashion, it requires the banking system to have ample reserves. An ample reserve environment means banks have more than enough reserves to meet their short-term money needs with plenty left over to be sensitive to the Fed raising or lowering the IORB. To set rates in this fashion, the Fed has had to dramatically increase both the amount of reserves in the banking system and the size of its balance sheet.

For example, total reserves held by banks historically averaged $50 billion or less. As the Fed started paying interest on reserves in 2008, that number jumped to nearly $1 trillion. It reached a peak of almost $4.2 trillion during the COVID-19 pandemic. By mid-2025, it had declined but remained elevated at more than $3.3 trillion.

Maintaining an ample reserve environment also requires the Fed to maintain a balance sheet significantly larger than under the previous interest rate setting regime. To generate sufficient reserves, the Fed purchases bonds from financial institutions, which increases the size of the Fed’s balance sheet. Historically, the Fed’s balance sheet stood at less than $900 billion. It ballooned to more than $2 trillion during the global financial crisis, only to be dwarfed by a fourfold increase to nearly $9 trillion during the pandemic. In mid-2025, the Fed’s balance sheet stood at roughly $6.6 trillion.

Such large amounts of money hanging on the sidelines of the banking system can quickly lead to inflationary pressures once banks choose to lend those reserves instead of keeping them at the Fed. Combined with the Fed’s large balance sheet, the current system raises concerns about whether the central bank’s interest rate policies lead to either financial repression or credit allocation—areas typically deemed inappropriate for monetary authorities to engage.

How the Federal Funds Rate Affects the Economy

Although the Fed primarily targets and influences the overnight rate of interest, its actions affect other interest rates because they are either indexed to, or built upon, the overnight rate. Additionally, investors shift their holdings of debt and their interest rate expectations based on what the Fed does and signals it will do with the FFR.

Three important consumer interest rates broadly affected by Federal Reserve interest rate policy are the thirty-year mortgage rate, credit card interest rates, and the interest rate on vehicle loans (Figure 2). Commercial and corporate borrowing are also affected by changes in the FFR target.

Figure 2: Fed Funds, 30-Year Mortgage, 4-Year Auto, & Credit Card Rates Since 2000

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED Graph

Interest rate changes have many effects on the economy. They change the relative cost for purchases that involve substantial amounts of debt, such as cars and houses. For example, even if there is no change in the sticker price of a $20,000 car, the car can become much more expensive if the buyer uses financing.

If the buyer finances the $20,000 at 5 percent amortized over five years, he will have a monthly payment of $377. But if he finances the $20,000 at 8 percent, his monthly payment will be $406 – a 7.4 percent increase. Although $29 a month may not seem like much, it adds more than $1,700 to the total cost over five years. If you add a zero—a $200,000 mortgage versus a $20,000 car loan—the increased financing cost becomes $290 per month or $3,480 per year. With a 30-year mortgage, that amounts to an increase of more than $100,000.

Interest rates affect the cost of purchasing homes and cars, credit cards, and business loans. Beyond that, they serve as important signals and prices for businesses and investors, acting as a key input for determining the costs and potential returns of entrepreneurial ventures. A slight change can make all the difference between a worthwhile endeavor and a failed one. With many interest rates influenced by the FFR, small changes to the Fed’s FFR target can impact large segments of the economy.

Conclusion

People watch the Fed closely because its decision to change its FFR target affects financial markets and the economy in many ways. Overall, the Fed’s current interest rate setting system has given the appearance of precise control, using IORB and ON RRP to manage the amount of money in the economy. But it has not been without costs. Banks and financial institutions are more dependent on the Fed and its decisions when it comes to how much lending and borrowing takes place in the economy. The current system also requires a large Fed balance sheet and higher levels of reserves. The result is a much larger Fed footprint in the economy that can dampen the efficient allocation of credit as determined by markets and poses risks to price stability—as evidenced by inflation hitting a 40-year high post-pandemic in 2022.
