data.title: CarMax Turns Into An Car Crash After Quarterly Results
data.description:
Thesis: KMX’s poor quarterly results took a big bite out of the company’s valuation. Following the steep decline, I suspect share prices are bottoming out and may recover a bit. It is, in my opinion, too late to sell, although if the economy deteriorates or we see evidence of further auto loan delinquencies rising, share prices could contract further.

Unfortunately, poor quarterly results have turned CarMax, Inc. (NYSE:KMX) into my worst stock recommendation of the year. CarMax’s shares have taken a deep dive following the release of Q2 2026 results. In premarket trading, stock prices were already down double digits, and shortly out of the gate, share prices had plummeted roughly 25%. Comparable-store sales for used vehicles were down 6.3% in Q2, while used retail units overall were down 5.4%. All told, used retail unit revenue was down 7.2% YOY.

Still, EPS also took a big hit, declining nearly 25% from a year prior. With share prices declining by a similar amount, the PE valuation is correcting in line with decreased performance. Encouragingly, YTD (fiscal year 2026) is still up 11% YOY, topping $2. Ultimately, the most important question, in my opinion, is whether the poor quarterly performance was largely due to a temporary pothole in the road owing to the pull-forward effect potentially caused by the trade war, or if it’s part of a sustained deterioration.

It wasn’t all bad news, with gross profits per unit in line with last year’s Q2 results. The gross profit per retail used unit weighed in at $2,216, while the wholesale unit gross profit was $993, and the Extended Protection Plan margin per retail unit came in at $576. Still, the retail used vehicle gross profit declined 7.6% YOY. Further, Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) expenses declined 1.6% to $601.1 million, and KMX is looking to trim a further $150 million over the next 18 months.

Loans Remain My Chief Concern

KMX keeps a lot of its loans on its books, and as I noted in my last write-up on the company, deteriorating loan quality could emerge as a serious threat. We have seen subprime auto loan delinquencies tick up in recent months, and it’s possible conditions will deteriorate. Auto finance loan income decreased 11.2% in Q2, falling to $102.6 million. The provision for loan losses jumped from $112.6 million a year ago to $142.2 million this year. The 2022 and 2023 loan vintages are currently the worst performers but do remain “highly profitable,” according to CarMax. The company tightened underwriting standards in April 2024, and so far, loans generated afterward remain on track with expectations.

In total, the allowance for loan losses of $507.3 million hit 3.02% of auto loans held for investment as of August 31, 2025, an increase from 2.76% in May 31, 2025. The large portfolio of auto loans and risk of rising delinquencies were my chief concerns when I last reported on KMX back in early August. The Q2 results certainly don’t allay the fears, although it is at least a bit encouraging that underwriting tightening is leading to improved performance.

What About Other Car Companies?

I haven’t yet really begun to look through the data, but what about other car companies? General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Ford (NYSE:F) both stumbled in early morning trading. I can’t say for sure that it was due to the issues with CarMax, but I doubt its struggles helped. Then there’s Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) and AutoNation (NYSE:AN), which have been gaining ground in recent weeks. They also suffered a rough go out of the gate. I wouldn’t necessarily tell investors to sell based on CarMax’s struggles, but it’d be wise to do further research.

As shown above, CNVA and AN both suffered some morning trading losses, although the declines were relatively moderate, at least compared to the sharp retreat suffered by KMX. Unlike CarMax, Carvana sells off many of its loans to investors. This should help reduce risks for Carvana if auto loan delinquencies continue to rise. I am not sure about AutoNation, but I would advise AN investors to take a close look at the company’s position in terms of auto loans.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Right now, I am thinking that CarMax’s struggles are part of a larger trend that will impact the economy as a whole. The cool-off is, in my opinion, likely due to the pull-forward effect of tariffs evaporating and leading to cooling demand. That said, KMX’s struggles could be due to deeper issues, and it’s possible that the company's struggles will continue apace or even accelerate in the weeks and months ahead.

The second quarter GDP results have been revised upward, showing significant growth. By and large, I believe the economy did do well. I also suspect we’ll see the economy slow a bit in the third quarter, and believe that there’s a very high chance the economy will continue to slow going forward. However, overall, I have been feeling confident that the economy will continue to expand. If the pull-forward effect was stronger than I previously realized, and that led to inflated expectations, it’s possible the market could be due for a correction even if the economy continues to grow.

CarMax’s performance alone doesn’t prove these worries correct, but I will be paying closer attention. Other issues, such as a historically high Shiller CAPE ratio, are also concerning. I am not hitting the panic button, but the market does feel more unstable than it did a month ago.

Key Takeaway: Sales Breakdown Is A Major Concern

The quarterly results weren’t good. There’s not really a major silver lining here as far as I can see. Efforts to cut costs will help in the long run, but that’s a potential future tailwind, not something we can currently mark in the books. CarMax is probably close to bottoming out unless another major headwind emerges. I wouldn’t rush to sell right now, but if you worry that CarMax’s stumble could portend issues with other companies, I’d suggest digging deeper.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Too Much Uncertainty After Grasberg Incident
data.description:
As investors we often tend to neglect certain idiosyncratic risks that are both rare and hard to predict. The recent event at Freeport-McMoRan's (NYSE:FCX) Grasberg Block Cave mine (GBC) in Indonesia is a perfect example of just that and unfortunately it is an incident that came with the loss of human life too.

According to FCX management, on September 8, around 800,000 metric tons of mud entered the mine and went through various mine levels. So far two fatalities have been identified with five more people still missing.

A force majeure has been declared after what was supposedly a mine landslide as rescue operations continue and the Indonesian government is now in the process of investigating the cause of the accident.

What that means is that there are still lots of uncertainties related to the future of the mine, and that is why FCX's stock price reacted by falling 17% yesterday after a press release regarding the accident was released.

This is a stark reminder that mining operations, especially those conducted underground, come with significant risks for anyone involved. The incident would also raise questions regarding safety and the need for companies to reinvest more of their profits back into the business as a way to reduce risk of accidents.

While it is too early to speculate about the overall impact of the event, there are a number of factors that should be taken into account.

Firstly, the Grasberg minerals district includes open-pit and three underground mines - the Grasberg Block Cave (GBC), the Deep Mill Level Zone (DMLZ) and Big Gossan. The incident occurred at GBC, which is by far the largest in terms of reserves.

According to the recent press release, the incident at GBC happened at one of the five production blocks, but damaged infrastructure needed to support other production areas at GBC.

The whole Grasberg district is quite large in terms of expected production, both for copper and gold.

The systemic importance of the Grasberg asset for FCX was highlighted during the last conference call and it should be kept in mind that as of Q2 2025 there were ongoing discussions with the Indonesian government about an extension of FCX operating rights.

Grasberg operations and district is our cornerstone asset. Second largest copper mine in the world. In this most recent quarter, we had a net credit for operating cost of $1 a pound. This asset has enabled us to create the modern Freeport. With the smelter nearing completion, we are progressing our discussions with the Indonesian government about extending our operating rights beyond 2041.

Source: FCX Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

The impact on FCX Q3 2025 results seems to be relatively low with anticipated 4% drop of copper sales and 6% for gold.

For the third quarter of 2025, FCX’s consolidated sales are expected to be approximately 4% lower for copper and approximately 6% lower for gold than July 2025 estimates.

Source: FCX Website

Having said that, however, we should keep in mind that the accident occurred in September, which is the last month of Q3. More importantly, however, there's still significant uncertainty regarding the reopening of the different blocks at GBC and other underground mines. Some scenarios were provided by FCX's management (see the extract below), but the final outcome is still uncertain.

Currently, PTFI expects the unaffected Big Gossan and Deep MLZ mines could restart operations by mid-fourth quarter 2025, with a phased restart and ramp-up of the GBC mine beginning in the first half of 2026. As a result, PTFI fourth quarter 2025 sales of copper and gold would be insignificant (previously estimated sales of 445 million pounds of copper and 345,000 ounces of gold).

In the first half of 2026, a phased restart and ramp-up of GBC could, initially commence in three production blocks - “PB2” and “PB3”, followed by a third production block “PB1S” in the second half of 2026 and the balance of “PB1C” in 2027. This schedule would target the return to pre-incident estimates in 2027.

Under this phased restart and ramp-up scenario, which is subject to a number of factors and could change, PTFI production in 2026 could potentially be approximately 35% lower than pre-incident estimates (previous production estimates for 2026 approximated 1.7 billion pounds of copper and 1.6 million ounces of gold).

Source: FCX Website

For these reasons, it might be too early to accurately estimate the overall impact of the disaster, but investors should also keep in mind that Indonesia Operations represent a significant portion of FCX's revenue and operating income.

What To Expect?

Even if we ignore the potential impact on FCX's share price from the recent events, the stock was already showing signs that it is not following the price of copper. For example, copper futures are up significantly over the past year, while FCX has been lagging behind.

This gap is now likely to widen further due to FCX's profitability headwinds and the fact that the market has reacted to a potential supply disruption stemming from the closure of the second largest copper mine in the world.

I covered all that in further detail about a year ago, when I showed why investors have been unimpressed with FCX's quarterly results and why all eyes have been focused on expected movements in the price of copper. Some of the positives that I noted were Indonesia's expansion and some progress being made on reducing costs. The former is now hardly a reason to get excited about FCX's future returns.

Moreover, it seems that the market does not fully account for the now expected drop in profitability of FCX. Although there is no solid relationship between margins and the price/sales multiple on a historical basis, the current multiple of above two does not seem to suggest a more meaningful drop in EBITDA margins in the coming quarters.

All that creates the necessary conditions for FCX to remain under pressure for the time being. The Non-GAAP earnings multiples (on a historical and forward basis) are also elevated even though forward estimates have not been updated yet to reflect the recent earnings release.

Investor Takeaway

The Indonesian mine incident is a stark reminder of the risks involved in mining operations, especially in extremely large underground projects. Based on the available information, it seems that H2 2025 results will be under significant pressure with uncertainties likely to linger into 2026 as well. For the time being it seems prudent to stay away from the stock as more details come in and the overall impact is being quantified. The level that FCX now trades at also does not suggest that the market is fully pricing-in the significant risks that lie ahead.

data.title: Forge Global: Favorable View Of Bank Alliance And Profitability Prospects
data.description:
My rating for Forge Global Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:FRGE) remains a "Buy." I think it will turn EBITDA-positive in '26. Its latest cooperation with a financial services organization is a wise move.

FRGE's "Intellectual Property" or "IP" commercialization and business acquisition were detailed in my earlier July 14, 2025, write-up.

Positive On Recent Collaboration With Tech-Focused Financial Institution

FRGE established an alliance with "Silicon Valley Bank" ("SVB") in 3Q25. According to the announcement, this involves the recommendation of "Forge’s private liquidity solutions" to the latter's non-listed depositors/borrowers to "manage their cap table, diversify their investor" mix.

The Type Of Enterprises That SVB Works With

As per the chart presented above, FRGE's newly added partner has a growing client base comprising of the leading "fintech" and "AI" businesses.

I think the firm would now have an additional channel to promote its services. In its 10-K, Forge highlighted that "our growth requires continued adoption of our (private market) platform by new customers and increased usage by current customers."

Also, the group should have gained market share because of this latest development.

It disclosed at Oppenheimer's (OPY) "28th Annual Technology, Internet & Communications Conference" (S&P Capital IQ transcript) last month that SVB "had a previous relationship with one of our (Forge's) competitors." The bank eventually switched to FRGE with an "exclusive partnership."

Imagine a privately held company intends to help its staff cash out their shares. Forge will be the only one referred, if the former turned to "Silicon Valley Bank" for advice.

Separately, some of FRGE's existing platform companies also borrow from SVB. This opens up cross-selling opportunities.

The Key Features For Its "Next Generation Marketplace" Or "NGM"

Forge plans to embed the above-mentioned NGM solution into its collaborators' systems. "Next Generation Marketplace" is described by the company as an upgraded "platform" to "execute private market trades," which was created with "partner integration" in mind.

For example, it will be very convenient for SVB's enterprise users to access information on private share transactions with their digital banking accounts.

This translates into incremental data-related topline contributions for FRGE in the near term. In my mid-July piece, I noted that its high-single-digit percent of "revenues (generated) from the data business" was still low compared to Intercontinental Exchange's (ICE) 25%.

Looking ahead, it can use NGM-integration as a selling point to get more banks on board to play a similar connector role as "Silicon Valley Bank." Financial firms would be happy to increase user stickiness with an expanded all-in-one online platform.

Profitability Turnaround Next Year Is A Realistic Goal

My July 14 article mentioned that FRGE's listed comparables "SoFi Technologies (SOFI), ICE, and Coinbase (COIN)" boasted relatively more demanding price-to-sales, or P/S, multiples. This is still the case.

Forge has been EBITDA-negative in every single quarter following its March '22 IPO. It has been penalized with a modest 2x P/S ratio.

Intercontinental, COIN, and SOFI are all profitable at the EBITDA level. They are deservingly trading in the 9x-11x price-to-revenue range.

I'm encouraged by the management's comments at the August Oppenheimer event that they have "a commitment to driving that (2026 EBITDA) breakeven."

Its EBITDA loss shrank from 1Q25's -$8.9 million to 2Q2025's -$5.4 million. The consensus estimate was wider at -$8.7 million. It has been moving in the right direction.

FRGE hasn't specified whether it will be EBITDA-positive in one of the quarters for FY26 or the full-year period. But my take is that this should happen sooner rather than later for a number of reasons.

One factor is the scaling up of its operations in Europe.

The group derived 7% of its FY2024 turnover from non-U.S. regions. It's reasonable to assume that the international segment was unprofitable last year because of negative operating leverage.

At OPY's investment forum in the prior month, FRGE indicated, "our ("cash") burn in that investment in Europe will have been extinguished coming out of 2025," as per prevailing "volume" trends.

My prediction is that the firm's European division should witness better profitability in the subsequent year with an enlarged sales base to absorb non-variable expenses.

Another thing to consider is the consolidation of its new acquiree's financials.

I drew attention to FRGE's "takeover of Accuidity Capital Management, or ACM, "a specialized asset management firm focused on private market investing," in my July-2025 update.

Forge has guided that the ACM transaction, which was done in early-July, is "accretive to EPS." This isn't a surprise, as fund managers typically earn high-margin recurring fee income.

Also, it's worth looking at the impact of FRGE's cost management initiatives.

The company shared at the conference organized by OPY that it has realized "efficiency gains" by shifting "some of our offshoring technology development in-house."

Given the specialized nature of Forge's business, which adds complexity, the firm could have paid a premium to engage third-party IT vendors. It's good to know that FRGE can do some of these on its own and produce savings at the same time.

You Would Watch These Risks

FRGE's relationship with SVB might turn sour, and the expected synergies may not be realized. This can deter Forge from cooperating with others in the same way.

The stock's valuation discount to peers could persist, assuming that its EBITDA-breakeven timeline is pushed backwards.

My Closing Remarks

FRGE is still a "Buy" in my opinion.

I'm positive on its operating profit prospects and the latest partnership with a bank.

Its capital appreciation potential is +60% (3.2/2.0), assuming mean reversion. Its current and 3-year average P/S metrics are 2.0x and 3.2x, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ data.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Strong Economic Reports Dent Rate Cut Hopes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Three strong economic reports give the Fed doves pause. (0:15) CarMax gets clobbered. (1:55) High-flying Oracle gets a rare bear. (2:18)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, this morning’s economic data just threw up a big speed bump on the easy money expressway. Initial jobless claims, durable goods orders and the final GDP revision all came in hawkish.

Wells Fargo economists said: “A data deluge this morning gives the U.S. economy a new lease on life.”

“Second quarter GDP, in hindsight, is stronger than it first appeared in light of robust consumer service spending and more robust business fixed investment,” they said. “August durable goods orders suggest the cap-ex momentum carried into Q3. Jobless claims fell for both first-time filers and those re-filing for existing benefits.”

Q2 GDP was perhaps the most eye-catching, even though that’s well in the rearview mirror. At the final print, the economy rose at +3.8% pace, up from the prior estimate of +3.3%.

Prices were also revised higher. The core PCE price index, which excludes food and energy, was revised up to 2.6% from 2.5% in the second estimate.

August durable goods orders rose 2.9%, compared with the -0.3% decrease expected and -2.7% in the prior month. Excluding transportation, new orders gained 0.4% last month, confounding expectations for a 0.1% decline.

And weekly jobless claims dropped unexpectedly to 218,000, well below the 238,0000 consensus and 232,000 the week before. Continuing claims dipped to 1.926 million from 1.928 million, lower than the 1.93 million consensus.

Following the data, the expected amount of Fed cuts this year fell to 38 bps from about 43 bps before the numbers hit. Treasury yields continued their recent march higher, with the 10-year yield (US10Y) touching 4.2% for the first time since the start of the month. And the dollar index (DXY) hit a three-week-high.

Among active stocks, CarMax (KMX) is plunging after falling short of consensus estimates with its second-quarter earnings report.

The company saw revenue decline 6% to $6.59 billion during what it said was a challenging quarter. That tally missed the consensus mark by $430 million. Total retail used vehicle unit sales decreased 5.4% to 199,729 compared to a year ago.

Oracle (ORCL) caught a Sell rating from Rothschild & Co Redburn.

Despite Oracle's role in the massive Stargate Project with OpenAI, the Rothschild says the market is overestimating the value of Oracle's contracted cloud revenue. It set a price target on the stock of $175, which is about 40% less than its current value.

And Rosenblatt analyst Chris Brendler started coverage on digital retail trading platform Webull (BULL) with a Buy rating, pointing to its international expansion and as well as a friendlier U.S. crypto regulatory environment.

"Crypto trading peaked at 20% of Webull's revenuesin the last cycle, and while it likely won't approach this level anytime soon, crypto is a key driver of our above-consensus estimates," Brendler said.

In other news of note, Aldi is making another aggressive move in the U.S. grocery sector by rebranding its private label portfolio and launching its first-ever namesake brand. The rebrand will take place over the next few years. The Germany-founded company said the move is a direct response to customer feedback.

More than 90% of Aldi products are private label. Several brands will be replaced with the Aldi name, while brands like Clancy’s, Simply Nature and Specially Selected will remain on shelves with modernized branding. Other items like “Red Bag Chicken” will adopt shopper-given nicknames.

Aldi's core strategy focuses on offering low prices through private labels, efficiency, and cost controls. The narrow focus has helped to boost Aldi's global brand recognition in the era of inflation-weary consumers.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA strategist Savita Subramanian says the S&P 500 (SP500) is now trading as if it were the new risk-free rate.

The index trades rich on 19 out of 20 metrics, with four key measurements – market cap to GDP, price to book, price to operating cash flow and enterprise value to sales – hitting all-time highs.

“Buying stocks at these multiples feels bad, but there are good ways (sales/EPS/GDP booms) and bad ways (price declines) to resolve this seemingly untenable situation,” she said.

The index now features companies with “a lower debt to equity ratio than prior decades,” reducing one traditional source of market risk.

“Perhaps we should anchor to today’s multiples as the new normal rather than expecting mean reversion to a bygone era,” Subramanian said.
